coredata.dc:title: Significantly improved high-temperature capacitive performance in polymer dielectrics utilizing ultra-small carbon quantum dots with Coulomb-blockade effect
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Hangchuan
Wang, Rui
Gou, Bin
Fu, Jing
Zhu, Yujie
Yang, Hao
Zhou, Jiangang
Li, Manxi
Zhong, An
Zhang, Daoming
Xu, Huasong
Bi, Chunhui
Xie, Congzhen
coredata.dc:description: Advanced electronic equipment requires polymer film capacitors to work under harsh temperature and electric field conditions. However, the inevitable deterioration of the insulation performance with electric field and temperature will limit the improvement of high-temperature capacitive performance. Herein, we report a novel sandwich-structured dielectric nanocomposite, utilizing microwave radiation in-situ to synthesize subminiature carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in the middle layer. Comprehensive simulation and experimental results confirm that CQDs, as an electronic barrier, prevent carriers from passing through the interlayer, thereby significantly reducing the leakage current in the polymer matrix at high temperatures, and obtaining excellent breakdown strength. Thanks to the slightly increased dielectric constant and significantly reduced energy loss, the nanocomposites with the optimal loading exhibit an excellent discharged energy density of 4.10 J cm−3 with efficiency above 90 % at 200 °C, which is far superior to most current dielectric nanocomposites. We believe that this simple and economic strategy provides a new idea for the development of green dielectric capacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coulomb-blockade effect
In-situ microwave carbonization
High-temperature dielectric capacitor
Energy storage density

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)Synthesis reaction equation of CQDs. (b) Diagram of Coulomb-blockade effect. (c) Schematic of the introduced CQDs molecular traps. The electron trap (φ e) can be calculated by φ e = EA mt – EA p, where EA mt and EA p are the electron affinities of the CQDs molecular and the polymer, respectively. (d) Band diagram of CQDs and PEI. Three-dimensional electrostatic potential of CQDs.

Leakage current density of (a) s-PEI/CQDs and (b) m-PEI/CQDs at 200 °C. (c) Temperature-dependent electrical conductivity of s-PEI/CQDs-4 and PEI under various electric fields. (d) Activation energy of s-PEI/CQDs-4 and PEI under various electric field. (e) Thermally stimulated depolarization current (TSDC) curves of PEI, m-PEI/CQDs-3 and s-PEI/CQDs-4. (f) Comparison of high trap energy level and highest trapped charge quantity among PEI, m-PEI/CQDs-3 and s-PEI/CQDs-4.
Simulation of electric field distribution and electrical tree propagation path of (a) the pristine PEI and (b) the s-PEI/CQDs-4 at 200 °C under 500 MV/m.

Field-dependent U d and η of (a) s-PEI/CQDs and (b) m-PEI/CQDs at 200 °C. (c) Comparison of the maximum U d above 90 % and E b among the s-PEI/CQDs at 10 Hz and 100 Hz. (d) Radar performance comparison map of s-PEI/CQDs-4 and PEI at 200 °C. (e) Comparison of the maximum discharged energy density as a function of the nanofiller content of s-PEI/CQDs-4 and previous references at 200 °C. (f) Cycling stability of the s-PEI/CQDs-4 and PEI at 200 MV m−1 and 200 °C.

(a) Characteristic breakdown strength and the retention rate with increasing the electrode diameter at 200 °C of s-PEI/CQDs-4 (0.07 vol% CQDs). (b) Characteristic breakdown strength and discharged energy density tested at different regions (as marked in the inset photograph) of the large s-PEI/CQDs-4 at 200 °C. (c) Unipolar and bipolar curves field-dependent U d and η of of s-PEI/CQDs-4 at 200 °C. (d) Stress − strain curves of PEI and s-PEI/CQDs-4.

head.ce:title: Significantly improved high-temperature capacitive performance in polymer dielectrics utilizing ultra-small carbon quantum dots with Coulomb-blockade effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electric Power Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510641, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electric Power Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510641, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Power Systems, Department of Electrical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Power Systems

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Power Systems, Department of Electrical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Electric Power Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510641, China (R. Wang).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Advanced electronic equipment requires polymer film capacitors to work under harsh temperature and electric field conditions. However, the inevitable deterioration of the insulation performance with electric field and temperature will limit the improvement of high-temperature capacitive performance. Herein, we report a novel sandwich-structured dielectric nanocomposite, utilizing microwave radiation in-situ to synthesize subminiature carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in the middle layer. Comprehensive simulation and experimental results confirm that CQDs, as an electronic barrier, prevent carriers from passing through the interlayer, thereby significantly reducing the leakage current in the polymer matrix at high temperatures, and obtaining excellent breakdown strength. Thanks to the slightly increased dielectric constant and significantly reduced energy loss, the nanocomposites with the optimal loading exhibit an excellent discharged energy density of 4.10 J cm−3 with efficiency above 90 % at 200 °C, which is far superior to most current dielectric nanocomposites. We believe that this simple and economic strategy provides a new idea for the development of green dielectric capacitors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dielectric capacitors with ultra-fast charging and discharging characteristics are critically important energy storage components in advanced electronic equipment and power systems [1–5]. Polymer dielectrics, compared with ceramic materials, are regarded as the preferred materials for film capacitors due to their high dielectric strength and graceful failure mechanism [6–9]. However, poor temperature stability forces polymer capacitors to be equipped with large volume temperature cooling systems, which challenges the miniaturization of electronic equipment in some emerging applications, such as hybrid electric vehicles, oil & gas exploration, and advanced propulsion devices [1–5]. At present, the most advanced commercially available dielectric polymers (biaxially oriented polypropylene, BOPP) can only work stably at temperatures below 100 °C [10,11]. Therefore, high glass transition temperature (T g) polymers (such as poly(ether imide), PEI) have received extensive attention for high-temperature application [12,13]. Unfortunately, the conductivity of PEI containing conjugated aromatic rings increase exponentially with the electric field and temperature, which will generate inevitable waste heat and even lead to equipment failure [14–16].

Doping insulation inorganic fillers into polymer matrix has been proved to be an effective strategy for curbing the conductivity loss at high temperatures [11,15]. The general insight of this strategy is to utilize the wide energy bandgap (E g) of inorganic filler as a dielectric barrier to block carrier migration or as a deep trap to capture free space charges [13]. For example, Ren et al. found that the incorporation of HfO2 with medium dielectric constant and wide bandgap increased the dielectric constant, while reducing the high-field leakage current density of PEI nanocomposites [17]. Furthermore, Cheng et al. plated Al2O3 and other inorganic materials on the surface of polymer by magnetron sputtering, which significantly improve the charge injection barrier between the electrode/dielectric, thereby reducing the leakage current under high temperature and high electric field [18]. This deposition method avoids the issue of dispersion in traditional nanocomposites, but still meets a huge challenge to achieve scalable production due to complex processes and high-cost [19].

Electrically conductive particles have an interesting quantum effect, i.e., the Coulomb-blockade effect [20–21]. When the size of a quantum dot is small enough, the capacitance C can be as small as 10-16F. Under this condition, whenever a single electron tunnels into the quantum dot, the additional charging energy e 2/C (e is the electronic charge) added to the quantum dot can be much greater than the thermal motion energy kT (k is the Boltzmann constant, T is the absolute temperature). Therefore, once an electron tunnels into the quantum dot, it will prevent the subsequent second electron from tunnelling. Utilizing this effect, more recently, Li et al. sputtered Au nanodots with Coulomb-blockade effect in a sandwiched polymer nanocomposites to capture the injected charges and block carrier transport and achieved an ultra-high discharged energy density of 6.25 J cm−3 only 0.0035 vol% at 150 °C [20]. The Coulomb-blockade effect of metal particles is directly related to temperature, e.g., the size of metal particles should be reduced from 10 nm to 2 nm to maintain Coulomb-blockade effect with the temperature raising from room temperature to 200 °C [21]. However, the precise control of metal particle size is an insurmountable challenge due to the limitations of process conditions, which restrains the application at higher temperatures. Therefore, it is urgent to find a simple, economical and size controllable method to achieve polymer dielectrics with excellent high temperature energy storage performance [20,22,23].

In this work, we report a novel sandwich-structured PEI nanocomposites with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in-situ synthesized in the intermediate layer. With green and environment-friendly organic materials as raw materials, i.e., citric acid (CA) and ethylene diamine (EDA), we draw upon microwave irradiation technology to synthesize CQDs with an average size of only 1.86 nm in polymer solution, which has excellent dispersion in polymer matrix (Fig. S1, Supporting Information). Compared to metal particles (generally obtained through magnetron sputtering or reduction of precious metal salts), CQDs have the advantages of simpler synthesis, more economical raw materials, and better size control. Benefited from the Coulomb-blockade effect of CQDs in the intermediate layer, the leakage current of the nanocomposites at high temperatures is significantly suppressed. At the optimum load of CQDs, a high discharged energy density (4.10 J cm−3 with efficiency above 90 %) is achieved at 200 °C and is almost an order of magnitude higher than that of the pristine PEI (1.35 J cm−3).

CQDs were in-situ synthesized by citric acid and ethylenediamine in PEI solution (Fig. 1 ), which was conducive for CQDs uniformly dispersing in PEI matrix. The structural characteristics of CQDs were confirmed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Figs. S2 and S3, Supporting Information). The HOMO/LUMO energy level and bandgap of CQDs can be determined by ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, which are −4.65 eV, −6.8 eV and 2.15 eV, respectively (Fig. S4 and S5, Supporting Information). Note that the transmission electron microscope (TEM) results show that the size of CQDs is controlled around 1.8 nm and uniformly dispersed in PEI matrix (Fig. S6, Supporting Information). The ultra-small quantum size and bandgap support them possessing the Coulomb-blockade effect at high temperature. The schematic of Coulomb-blockade effect is shown in Fig. 1 b, i.e., the first electron tunneled into Coulomb Island will block the entry of the next electron as K b T < e 2/2C [23–30]. In addition, there is a 1.87 eV molecular trap between CQDs and PEI to prevent electron escape (Fig. 1 c and 1d), which further effectively block the migration of electrons at high temperature and high electric field [31–33]. The electrostatic potential distribution of CQDs is shown in Fig. 1 e. Multiple localized large electrostatic potentials on the surface imply strong electronic adsorption.

Moreover, we have designed a sandwich-structured film composed of PEI as the outer layer and PEI/CQDs nanocomposite as the inner layer, aiming to further improve the breakdown strength by the interlayer interface barrier. Sandwich-structured PEI/CQDs nanocomposites are named s-PEI/CQDs, and monolayer-structured PEI/CQDs are named m-PEI/CQDs. The cross-section of s-PEI/CQDs-4 was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and there are no obvious defects between adjacent layers, demonstrating excellent film quality (Fig. S7, Supporting Information).

The frequency-dependent dielectric spectra of s-PEI/CQDs and m-PEI/CQDs were recorded using a broadband dielectric spectrometer, as shown in Figs. S8 and S9, Supporting Information. While the CQDs exhibit marginal effect on the dissipation factor, the dielectric constant of dielectric polymers can be significantly improved by loading CQDs, e.g., 3.75 of s-PEI/CQDs-4 versus 3.20 of the pristine PEI at 200 °C and 1 kHz, which is conducive to achieving a higher energy storage density under the same electric field.

We then conducted a comprehensive high-field electrical characterization of all dielectric films to explain the impact of ultra-small size CQDs on charge transport. The statistical results of breakdown strength (E b) based on the two-parameter Weibull distribution indicate that the appropriate loading of CQDs is beneficial to improving the E b of dielectric films, and the sandwich-structured s-PEI/CQDs have larger enhancement compared with the monolayer-structured m-PEI/CQDs (Figs. S10 and S11, Supporting Information) [34–36]. For instance, the E b of s-PEI/CQDs-4 is 508.4 MV m−1 at 200 °C, which is 1.14 times and 1.08 times that of the PEI and m-PEI/CQDs-3, respectively. It should be noted that high loading of CQDs can actually cause a decrease in E b, mainly due to the formation of irregular large-sized aggregates at high concentrations of CQDs (Fig. S12, Supplementary Information), resulting in the failure of Coulomb-blockade effect and an increase of physically interface defects. In addition, compared with s-PEI/CQD, electrons can be directly injected into m-PEI/CQD from the electrode through quantum tunneling without overcoming the Schottky barrier of the electrode-polymer interface [18], as some CQDs in m-PEI/CQD have a small distance from the electrode. To reveal the impact of CQDs on improving E b of PEI, the leakage current density of all samples as a function of electric field was recorded at 200 °C (Fig. 2
a and 2b). Since the interlayer dielectric barrier and the Coulomb-blockade effect of CQDs, the leakage current density of s-PEI/CQDs-4 is one order of magnitude lower than that of the pristine PEI, e.g., at 300 MV m−1 and 200 °C, from 3.71 × 10-3 A m−2 of the pristine PEI to 3.37 × 10-4 A m−2 of s-PEI/CQDs-4. In addition, the leakage current density of all nanocomposites increases exponentially with the electric field, which means that hopping conduction is the main charge transfer mechanism in the material [37,38]. The hopping distance (λ) from the hopping conduction model is related to the trap distribution density. Compared to 1.22 nm of the pristine PEI and 1.31 nm of the m-PEI/CQDs-4, the s-PEI/CQDs-4 have the lowest λ (1.14 nm), demonstrating the largest trap density, and thereby enhancing the E b. The activation energy, which is associated to trap energy level, is determined by the temperature-conductivity plot under different electric fields, as shown in Fig. 2 c. Apparently, s-PEI/CQDs-4 exhibits significantly increased activation energy compared to the pristine PEI, e.g., s-PEI/CQDs-4 had activation energies of 0.36 eV and 0.75 eV at 100 MV m−1 and 400 MV m−1, respectively, verses 0.53 eV and 0.80 eV of the pristine PEI (Fig. 2 d). Moreover, the thermally stimulated depolarization current (TSDC) results further confirm that s-PEI/CQDs-4 has the deepest trap energy level, as the detrapping current peak shifts from 211.7 °C of m-PEI/CQDs-3 to 215.7 °C of s-PEI/CQDs-4 (Fig. 2 e). The optimized trap quantity and energy level are the main factors that result in the high-field insulation performance of s-PEI/CQDs-4 far superior to the original PEI and m-PEI/CQDs-3 (Fig. 2 f).

Furthermore, we simulated the development of electrical trees in PEI matrix and s-PEI/CQDs-4 through phase field calculations (Fig. 3 ). Note that although electrical trees do not occur in micrometer scale dielectric films, electrical tree branch simulation can effectively analyze the propagation path of charges in the film. Interestingly, compared to the pure PEI (Fig. 3 a), the CQDs in the intermediate layer of s-PEI/CQDs can effectively hinder the development of the electrical tree (Fig. 3 b), which explains the significantly enhanced breakdown strength of s-PEI/CQDs-4. This phenomenon can be explained by two reasons. Firstly, compared to PEI matrix, CQDs have high dielectric and conductivity, resulting in a low concentration of electric fields near them, which is beneficial for attracting the development path of electrical trees. Secondly, the Coulomb blocking effect of CQDs and the dielectric barrier formed by the dielectric mismatch between CQDs and PEI will hinder the continued development of the electrical tree. Therefore, both experimental and simulation results have demonstrated the key role of ultra-small size CQDs in limiting the transfer of charges in polymer matrix.

The capacitive energy storage performances of dielectric thin films, i.e., discharged energy density (U d) and charge–discharge efficiency (η), are obtained from the electric displacement-electric field (D-E) loops, as shown in Fig. S13 (Supporting Information). Clearly, under the same electric field, the electric displacement of the nanocomposites are higher than that of the pristine PEI (e.g., at 200 °C and 500 MV m−1, 1.77 μC cm−2 of s-PEI/CQDs-4 and 1.72 μC cm−2 of m-PEI/CQDs-3 versus 1.70 μC cm−2 of PEI) (Fig. S14, Supporting Information), consistent with the trend of dielectric constant (i.e., the values of s-PEI/CQDs-4, m-PEI/CQDs-3 and PEI are 3.75, 3.65, and 3.20, respectively) (Figs. S8-S9, Supporting Information). Note that s-PEI/CQDs exhibit significantly higher U d and η at temperatures ranging from room temperature to 200 °C (Figs. S15-S20, Supporting Information), mainly contributed to enhanced breakdown strength and suppressed leakage current. For example, at 200 °C, the U d at η > 90 % (U 90%) of s-PEI/CQDs-4 is 4.10 J cm−3, which is 3.03 times the pristine PEI (Fig. 4
a). In comparison, the U d and η of m-PEI/CQDs are poorer compared to s-PEI/CQDs (Fig. 4 b), which may be caused by electron tunneling between the surface layer CQDs and the electrode. To further determine that the improvement of energy storage performance of polymers by CQDs is owning to the Coulomb-blockade effect, we prepared polymer composites with carbon nanoparticles of 20–50 nm size as a comparison (Fig. S21, Supporting Information). The results indicate that when the Coulomb-blockade effect is disappeared, carbon nanoparticles with only trap effect will significantly reduce the breakdown strength and energy storage performance of the polymer (Fig. S22, Supporting Information), further emphasizing the importance of the Coulomb-blockade effect. Considering that low η means large waste heat, leading to thermal runaway of the capacitor, we defined the U 90% as comparable performance [39–42]. As shown in Fig. 4 c, the optimal U 90% of s-PEI/CQDs-4 at 100 Hz and 10 Hz is 4.12 J cm−3 and 2.41 J cm−3, respectively, which is much better than the pristine PEI (U 90% is 0.98 J cm−3 and 1.35 J cm−3, respectively, at 10 Hz and 100 Hz) [39,43]. Overall, s-PEI/CQDs-4 outperforms the PEI in all dielectric and high-temperature capacitive performances (Fig. 4 d). Compared to the dielectric energy storage performance reported in the previously literature, s-PEI/CQDs-4 also exhibit outstanding U 90% at 200 °C (Fig. 4 e). Particularly, the green synthesis process and extremely low loading of CQDs in s-PEI/CQDs-4 mean excellent processability and environmental friendliness [44–46].

The long-term charge–discharge cycle characteristic is an important factor for evaluating the service life of dielectric thin films. We have characterized s-PEI/CQDs −4 at 150 °C and 200 °C, respectively (Fig. 4 f and Fig. S23, Supporting Information). Under the electric field condition of 200 MV m−1 (which is slightly higher than the common operating electric field of hybrid electric vehicles), s-PEI/CQDs-4 exhibit negligible energy loss (the change of U d less than 3 % at 200 °C) after 50,000 cycles, and even slightly improve the η (i.e., from 95 % to 97 %), indicating the excellent capacitive performance stability of the dielectric film [47].

Generally, composites are plagued by performance fluctuations, we therefore have conducted a comprehensive evaluation of the film quality of s-PEI/CQDs-4. At 200 °C, as the electrode diameter increases from 3 mm to 20 mm, the breakdown strength of s-PEI/CQDs-4 still retains 83 %, which is equivalent to pure polymer PEI (Fig. 5
a). In addition, we prepared a large-area dielectric film (25 × 15 cm2) and compared the U d and E b in different regions (Fig. 5 b). Encouragingly, the variations in U d and E b in the nine regions are negligible, confirming the high uniformity of the s-PEI/CQDs-4 film. In addition, the U d and η obtained by a bipolar D-E loop are comparable to those obtained by a unipolar D-E loop (Fig. 5 c). The mechanical properties of s-PEI/CQDs-4 are further characterized to evaluate the ability to form roll-to-roll capacitors. Obviously, s-PEI/CQDs-4 have higher tensile strength and elongation at break than PEI (Fig. 5 d), demonstrating its applicability to flexible capacitors since the PEI has been proven to be capable of producing roll-to-roll capacitors [48].

The rapid discharge rate and high-power density of dielectrics are key parameters for the application of capacitors in power electronic equipment. Obviously, compared to the state-of-art commercial BOPP film (release 0.42 J cm−3 within 2.6 μs), s-PEI/CQDs-4 releases much higher energy density (0.68 J cm−3) in a shorter discharge time (1.85 μs), ultimately resulting in a power density of up to 0.35 MW cm−3 (Fig. S24, Supporting Information). The above results all confirm the superiority of s-PEI/CQDs-4 dielectric thin films for capacitors in high-temperature environments.

In summary, we have reported a novel strategy for improving the capacitive performance of dielectric polymers at elevated temperatures. Based on simple microwave radiation, CQDs with an ultra-small particle size of only 1.8 nm can be synthesized in polymer matrix, enabling the Coulomb-blockade effect at a high temperature of 200 °C and significantly inhibiting the migration of carries under high electric fields. Coupled with the macro sandwich structure, the breakdown strength and electrical insulation performance of the film are further optimized. Among the composites with the best composition, a discharged energy density of 4.10 J cm−3, with the charge–discharge efficiency above 90 % at 200 °C, has been achieved, which is superior to most nanocomposites. It is worth noting that the ultra-low loading of CQDs enables the composite dielectric polymer to possess extremely high film quality, addressing the inherent problem of performance fluctuations in nanocomposites. Simply preparation processes, environmental-friendly synthetic materials, and superior capacitive performances provide a new idea for the design of polymer dielectrics for high-temperature applications.

Materials: Citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology. Polyetherimide (PEI) pellets was provided by PolyK Technologies. 1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) solvent was supplied by Sigma Aldrich. All the chemicals were used as received unless otherwise specified.

Preparation of pristine PEI films: The pure PEI film is obtained through a simple solution casting method. Typically, 200 mg of PEI pellets were dissolved in 10 mL of NMP solution and stirred overnight. Then the solution was dropped onto a pre-cleaned glass substrate using a syringe with a filter and dried at 80 °C for 12 h. Finally, the temperature was raised to 250 °C for 60 min to enhance the bonding force between the film and the glass substrate.

Preparation of m-PEI/CQDs films: First, 200 mg of PEI pellets were dissolved in 10 mL of NMP solution and labelled solution A. Subsequently, NMP solutions containing 0.081 g CA and 29 μL EAD were prepared and labelled solution B [49]. Different volumes of solution B were added to solution A and then heated up to 140 °C through microwave irradiation (stirring, open batch, the power and frequency are 900 W and 2450 MHz, respectively.), provoking the evaporation of the NMP. The temperature was kept constant for 1 min; after that, the irradiation was stopped, and the mixture was filled to 10 mL of NMP. The same process was repeated two more times, for a total of 3 min at 140 °C. The solid product was yielding a brownish powder with a yield in mass of 35 %.

Preparation of sandwich structured s-PEI/CQDs nanocomposites: The sandwich structured nanocomposites were prepared by a hot-pressing process. The three individual layers of PEI, PEI/CQDs and PEI were stacked together and hot pressed at 80 °C at 10 MPa for 10 min. After that the films were heated at 250 °C for 60 min. The typical thickness of the sandwich-structured nanocomposite is 9–12 μm (Fig. S1a, Supporting Information).

Characterizations: Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, Japan), scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Merlin, Germany), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Supra+, UK), fourier transform infrared spectrum-photometer (FTIR, Nicolet IS50-Nicolet, USA), ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (Axis Supra+, UK) and UV–vis spectrometer (Lambda950, USA) were used to characterize the microscopic morphology, structure and composition of the samples. Before performing dielectric and electrical measurements, all the samples were sputter-coated with gold electrodes of 50 nm thickness and 3 mm diameter. The dielectric properties of the nanocomposites were determined using an Agilent E4980A test instrument ranging from 100 Hz to 1 MHz at RT, 150 °C and 200 °C. The leakage current was obtained using a Keithley 6517B Pico ammeter employing a Stanford PS350 voltage source to control the voltage. The dielectric breakdown strength was measured using PK-CPR1601 tester at a pre-set rate of 250 V s−1. The unipolar electric displacement-electric field (D-E) loops were recorded using a modified Sawyer-Tower circuit with a unipolar wave at 10 Hz and 100 Hz. The charge–discharge cycle experiments were measured using a PK-CPR1601 tester (PolyK Technologies, USA). Thermally stimulated depolarization current (TSDC) was measured using a Keysight B2985 electrometer according to the following experimental procedure: first, under a 50 MV m−1 DC electric field, the sample was polarized for 30 min at 200 °C, and thereafter rapidly cooled to 0 °C while maintaining the polarized electric field. After reaching a low temperature, the polarized electric field was cut off and the sample was short-circuited for 3 min. Finally, the sample was heated to 250 °C at a rate of 3 °C min−1, and the current change was measured using an electrometer. In-situ microwave carbonizes sandwich-structured PEI/CQDs nanocomposites with loading different CQDs volume content of 0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7 and 0.9 are denoted as PEI, s-PEI/CQDs-1, s-PEI/CQDs-2, s-PEI/CQDs-3, s-PEI/CQDs and s-PEI/CQDs-5, respectively. In-situ microwave carbonizes monolayer-structured PEI/CQDs nanocomposites with loading different CQDs volume content of 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7 and 0.9 are denoted as m-PEI/CQDs-1, m-PEI/CQDs-2, m-PEI/CQDs-3, m-PEI/CQDs-4 and m-PEI/CQDs-5, respectively. The calculation details of electrical tree simulation can be found in Supplementary Note 6 of the Supplementary Information.

The volume fraction of CQDs in PEI/CQDs composites can be calculated by the equation below,
(1)
CQDs vol\% =
m
( CA )
+
V
( EDA )
×
ρ
( EDA )
ρ
( CQDS )
÷
m
( PEI )
ρ
( PEI )
where m (CA) and V (EDA) are the amounts of CA and EDA added to the PEI solution, respectively. ρ (CQDs) is the density of CQDs, which is obtained as 1.83 g/mL through density meter testing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial and technical support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China grants 52307025 (R.W.) and 51977084 (C.Z.X.).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.146672.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Nature
